title: Lateral Shift as a Control Knob for Localization Transitions in a Quasiperiodic Ladder
authors: Bing Shao, Guangjie Zhang, Longwen Zhou, Jiangbin Gong, Weiwei Zhu
abstract: This work reports rich localization-delocalization transitions in a quasiperiodic ladder, of which the two legs are subject to the same quasiperiodic onsite potential but can be shifted laterally relative to each other. It is found that the lateral shift between the two legs effectively generates a magnetic flux in the reciprocal momentum space. The lateral shift thus offers a control knob, allowing us to access and simulate rich phenomena including magnetic-flux-enhanced localization, magnetic-flux-suppressed localization, and magnetic-flux-induced reentrant localization transitions. The underlying physical mechanisms as well as the phase boundaries separating localized, mixed, and extended phases are both qualitatively and quantitatively understood, based on a band-structure analysis that employs a commensurate approximation to the quasiperiodic potential, requiring only unit cells of small to modest sizes. Our work provides a highly tunable platform for exploring localization physics with promising applications such as quantum switching, and a broadly applicable approach for understanding localization-delocalization transitions in quasiperiodic systems.
summary: This paper demonstrates that shifting the legs of a quasiperiodic ladder relative to each other allows for the tuning of localization phases via a synthetic magnetic flux. It introduces a highly accurate, system-size-independent method for identifying phase transitions in quasiperiodic systems. The findings suggest a tunable platform for studying complex localization phenomena like reentrant transitions.
main_problem: Investigating how a lateral shift between two legs of a quasiperiodic ladder can act as a control knob to induce various localization-delocalization transitions.
main_result: The lateral shift effectively generates a synthetic magnetic flux in reciprocal space, enabling magnetic-flux-enhanced, suppressed, and reentrant localization transitions. The study also introduces a new, system-size-independent diagnostic called the anisotropic bandwidth ratio (Rj) to precisely identify phase boundaries.
method: The authors use a duality transformation to map the real-space ladder to a reciprocal-space Hamiltonian and employ a commensurate approximation using Fibonacci numbers to analyze the band structure.
model_or_system: A 1D quasiperiodic ladder where two legs are subject to the same quasiperiodic onsite potential but are laterally shifted relative to each other, effectively simulating a synthetic magnetic flux.
key_observables: Inverse Participation Ratio (IPR), Normalized Participation Ratio (NPR), Fractal Dimension (FD), eta diagnostic, and the anisotropic bandwidth ratio (Rj).
important_parameters: Lateral shift (q or phi), interleg coupling (kappa), quasiperiodic potential strength (v), and the inverse golden ratio (alpha).
main_assumptions: The use of a commensurate approximation to treat the irrational quasiperiodic potential as a periodic system with a finite unit cell.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the schematic of the ladder and its duality-transformed version with synthetic flux. Fig 2/4 shows phase maps and logarithmic plots of the Rj diagnostic (Rmin and Rmax) used to delineate extended, mixed, and localized phases.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the quasiperiodic ladder model, establishes the duality between lateral shift and synthetic magnetic flux, presents numerical results for various localization transitions, introduces the Rj diagnostic for phase boundary prediction, and concludes with potential applications.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Quasiperiodic ladders. (a) A quasiperiodic ladder with a lateral shift between its upper and lower legs. (b) The duality-transformed (DT) quasiperiodic ladder with an synthetic magnetic flux ϕ per plaquette. With the rational approximation F5/F6 = 5/8 to the irrational α parameter, the lattice becomes periodic with a unit cell of 8 sites.

FIG. 2. Flux-dependent localization. (a) Energy spectrum vs the effective magnetic flux ϕ, plotted with the fractal dimen- sion Γ of the eigenstates, for system parameters κ = 1.8 and 2t = 1. (b) Same as in (a) but for 2t = 2.5. (c) Phase dia- gram vs the quasiperiodic potential strength 2t and ϕ, with κ = 1.8, color-coded by the values of η. (d) Same as in (c), but for κ and ϕ, with 2t = 3.4. The system size is taken as L = F15 = 610. Green lines in panels (c) and (d) represent the theoretical analysis, in excellent agreement with the nu- merically found phase boundaries.

FIG. 3. Bandwidth under a commensurate approximation, with lattice constant lCA = F3 = 2. (a) Band structure for κ = 1.8, 2t = 1 and ϕ = 2. (b) Bandwidth of the first band W1 vs ϕ for κ = 1.8 and 2t = 1, with other parameters the same as in Fig. 2(a). (c) Bandwidth of the second band W2 vs ϕ for κ = 1.8 and 2t = 2.5, with other system parameters the same as in Fig. 2(b). Dashed lines in panels (b) and (c) indicate where the bandwidth (W1 and W2, respectively) equals the renormalized strength of quasiperiodic potential 2˜t = 2( √

FIG. 4. Anisotropic bandwidth ratio obtained under a commensurate approximation with lCA = F9 = 34. (a) Logarithmic map of the minimum anisotropic bandwidth ratio Rmin vs 2t and ϕ for κ = 1.8 as in Fig. 2(c). (b) Same as in (a), but for the maximal anisotropic bandwidth ratio Rmax vs 2t and ϕ. (c) Same as in (b), but for Rmax vs κ and ϕ for 2t = 3.4 as in Fig. 2(d). Black curves indicate where Rmin = 1 or Rmax = 1, plotted as green curves in Figs. 2(c) and 2(d).
